local_names.thai: ปลาไหลกรอบยำ

aliases: ปลาไหลกรอบยำ
Crispy Eel with Spicy Thai Dressing

english: Crispy Eel with Spicy Thai Dressing
thai: ปลาไหลกรอบยำ

food_subgroup: Freshwater eel, deep-fried, served with Thai spicy salad dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crispy deep-fried eel with Thai salad dressing. FOODON_03302051 (eel food product) is the closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJK covers freshwater eel as a raw commodity. No specific code exists for the deep-fried preparation with Thai salad dressing; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for crispy eel with Thai salad. SNOMED CT does not provide granular prepared-dish codes for this item.

preparation_method: dressed with Thai spicy salad dressing
garnished with fresh herbs and chilli

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of crispy eel with Thai salad is approximately 200 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried eel pieces dressed with spicy Thai salad dressing and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) combined with estimation for deep-frying oil absorption and Thai salad dressing components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for crispy deep-fried eel with Thai salad dressing exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA FoodData Central entry for eel (mixed species, cooked, dry heat, FDC ID 175136) was used as the primary protein/fat/micronutrient anchor. An estimated 8–10% additional fat was added to account for deep-frying oil absorption (standard culinary adjustment per USDA cooking-loss factors). Thai salad dressing components (lime juice, fish sauce, palm sugar, chilli, herbs) contribute minor carbohydrate and sodium increments estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on mixed protein-fat-dominant seafood dish with minor carbohydrate from dressing.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy eel with Thai salad dressing. The dish is protein- and fat-dominant with low carbohydrate content (approximately 5.8 g per 100 g), primarily from palm sugar and lime juice in the dressing. A GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with other low-carbohydrate, protein-fat-dominant seafood dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The glycemic load per 200 g serving is calculated as (35 × 11.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.1, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g serving of this dish provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced fish sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load from protein- and fat-dominant composition
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of 4.1 per serving, this dish has a minimal acute impact on postprandial blood glucose and can be included in a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan in moderate portions, though the palm sugar in the dressing should be noted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat and sodium from deep-frying and fish sauce, partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids from eel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While eel provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin D, the deep-frying process increases total and saturated fat content, and the high sodium from fish sauce warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; grilled or steamed preparation would be preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Eel with Thai Salad (Thai: ปลาไหลกรอบยำ, romanized: Pla Lai Krob Yam) is a Thai dish consisting of freshwater eel that has been deep-fried until golden and crispy, then dressed with a vibrant Thai spicy salad dressing made from lime juice, fish sauce, palm sugar, fresh chilli, shallots, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and fresh herbs such as mint and coriander. The dish is served as a starter or sharing plate in Thai restaurants. The texture contrasts the crunchy fried eel exterior with the bright, tangy, and spicy dressing. In Singapore, this dish appears on the menus of upscale Thai restaurants such as The Upper House at Funan, where it is positioned as a premium appetiser at approximately SGD 25. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (14.2 g per 100 g) due to deep-frying, with relatively low carbohydrate content (5.8 g per 100 g) primarily from the palm sugar and lime juice in the dressing. Eel is a notable source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, omega-3 fatty acids, and phosphorus. The sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions.

palm sugar: น้ำตาลปี๊บ

confidence_reason: No exact entry for crispy deep-fried eel with Thai salad dressing was found in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central eel (cooked, dry heat) with culinary adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and Thai dressing components. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Fish and seafood' based on the primary ingredient being freshwater eel. 2. Food name canonicalised from 'Crispy Eel Temptation' (menu-style promotional name) to 'Crispy Eel with Thai Salad' as the canonical English descriptor; the original menu name is preserved as an alias. 3. Nutrient values are composite estimates; a direct laboratory analysis or an approved database entry for this specific dish would be required to upgrade confidence to high. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central eel data and are not confirmed by a direct dish-level measurement; these values are provided as best-available estimates. 5. Halal status is marked false because eel classification under Islamic dietary law varies by school of jurisprudence (permissible under Shafi'i but debated under Hanafi); consumers should verify with the serving restaurant. 6. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 fields have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA source data. All numeric fields are populated.
